1: A type: the victory catalog of all defeated kings. Chapter 12 is the book of Joshua's triumph-list — the systematic enumeration of every royal power brought low by YHWH through Israel. Paul’s declaration that God “disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, triumphing over them in him [Christ]” (Colossians 2:15) is the NT counterpart: the cross is the cosmic victory catalog where every spiritual power aligned against humanity is listed among the defeated. The catalog form itself is theologically significant — naming the defeated is the ancient victory-announcement that establishes the new king’s universal dominion.
2: A type: Sihon king of the Amorites, who lived at Heshbon. Sihon and Og (Joshua 12:4) are the two prototypical anti-kingdom rulers defeated east of the Jordan — the conquest’s first great victories (Numbers 21:21-35). The Psalms use these two defeats as paradigmatic examples of YHWH’s warrior-power on behalf of his people (Psalms 135:11; Joshua 136:19-20). Their defeat by Moses prefigures their listing here under Joshua — the conquest begun east of the Jordan is completed west of it, and Christ’s work follows the same pattern: the defeat of the enemy is inaugurated at the cross and completed at the return.
3: A type: the geographic extent of Sihon’s former domain — from the Arabah to the Sea of Kinnereth, from the Dead Sea to Beth-jeshimoth, southward to the slopes of Pisgah. The detail of the territorial boundaries signals that YHWH’s victory is total: not merely the capital but the entire sovereign domain of the defeated king has passed to the new suzerain. In Ephesians 1:20-21, Christ is seated “far above all rule and authority and power and dominion, and above every name that is named, not only in this age but also in the one to come” — the cosmic extent of his kingship leaves no territory outside his domain.
4: A type: Og king of Bashan, who lived at Ashtaroth and Edrei, one of the remnant of the Rephaim. Og of Bashan is the giant-king — the last of the Rephaim (Deuteronomy 3:11: “his bed was a bed of iron... nine cubits in length and four cubits in breadth”). The defeat of the greatest Rephaim king by Moses under YHWH’s command is a type of Christ’s conquest of what seems invincible: the powers that appear too large and entrenched to defeat. Hebrews 2:14-15 describes Christ’s incarnation as destroying “the one who has the power of death, that is, the devil, and deliver all those who through fear of death were subject to lifelong slavery” — the ultimate giant-king defeated.
5: A type: the geographic extent of Og’s territory — half Gilead to the border of Sihon. The Bashan territory (high plateau northeast of the Jordan) became proverbially associated with power and fertility (Psalms 22:12: “strong bulls of Bashan have encircled me”; Amos 4:1: “cows of Bashan” for the wealthy oppressors of Israel). Og’s domain encompassed sixty cities (Deuteronomy 3:4-5) — a significant concentration of fortified strength. That even Og’s extensive domain falls entirely under Moses’s conquest anticipates the Pauline claim that Christ’s dominion encompasses even the most entrenched and powerful spiritual territories.
6: A type: these two kingdoms Moses and YHWH’s servant defeated, and Moses gave their land as a possession to the Reubenites, Gadites, and half the tribe of Manasseh. Moses defeated the eastern kingdoms but did not enter Canaan to complete the western conquest — Joshua completes what Moses began. This is the most explicit typological point the book of Joshua makes: Moses (the law) cannot complete the full inheritance; Joshua (whose name means YHWH saves) completes the work. Hebrews 4:8 makes the same point about Jesus: if Joshua had given them the true rest, God would not have spoken later of another day. The completion of Moses’s work by Joshua is the type; the completion of the law’s preparatory work by Christ is the antitype.
7: A type: the list of kings defeated by Joshua west of the Jordan, which the LORD God of Israel gave to the tribes of Israel as their possession. The transition from eastern to western victories marks the full scope of the conquest: both sides of the Jordan, both the Mosaic and Joshuanic campaigns, fall under YHWH’s victory. The comprehensive formula “which YHWH the God of Israel gave to the tribes of Israel as a possession” frames every military victory as ultimately a divine gift — consistent with the holy-war theology throughout the book that YHWH is the true agent of victory. Christ’s cosmic victory is similarly a gift he shares with his people: “the meek shall inherit the earth” (Matthew 5:5) echoes the land-gift pattern.
8: A type: the catalog covers the hill country, lowland, Arabah, slopes, wilderness, and Negev — six distinct geographical zones covering the full geographic range of Canaan. The six-zone listing signals completeness of the conquest terrain, analogous to the listing of peoples (Hittites, Amorites, Canaanites, Perizzites, Hivites, Jebusites) representing the full human opposition. Colossians 1:16 describes the extent of Christ’s creation-lordship and victory: “all things were created through him and for him... whether thrones or dominions or rulers or authorities” — the comprehensive catalog of spiritual powers corresponds to the comprehensive territorial catalog of Joshua 12.
9: A type: the king of Jericho. Jericho is the first city taken, the firstfruits of the conquest, whose walls fell without military assault through the divine march and trumpet-shout. The king of Jericho is the first name on the western victory roster. The firstfruits principle governing Jericho’s total cherem looks forward to Christ as the firstfruits of resurrection (1 Corinthians 15:20-23): just as Jericho was the firstfruits of the land devoted entirely to YHWH, Christ’s resurrection is the firstfruits of the general resurrection — the first and defining victory that guarantees all subsequent victories.
10: A type: the king of Jerusalem. Jerusalem (then Jebus) is conquered by Joshua but not permanently held until David (2 Samuel 5:6-9). The king of Jerusalem is listed among the defeated — the city’s future as David’s capital, and ultimately as the city of Christ’s death and resurrection, is contained in seed form in this single entry. The city that will become the center of the entire biblical redemptive story is already named as a conquered domain in Joshua’s victory list. The new Jerusalem that descends from heaven in Revelation 21-22 is the eschatological fulfillment of the conquest claim first registered here.
11: A type: the king of Hebron. Hebron is Abraham’s city — the site of the Cave of Machpelah where Abraham, Sarah, Isaac, Rebekah, Jacob, and Leah are buried (Gen 23; Joshua 49:29-32). The conquest of Hebron claimed the patriarchal burial ground and the land Abraham purchased as Israel’s legal title to Canaan. Caleb will claim Hebron as his personal inheritance in chapter 14 — the most significant act of individual land-claiming in the book. David will be crowned king at Hebron (2 Samuel 2:11) before taking Jerusalem. Hebron’s listing here — king defeated, city claimable — is the typological foundation for all subsequent Hebronic events in Israel’s covenant story.
12: A type: kings of Jarmuth and Lachish, members of the southern coalition (Joshua 10:3). These two cities aligned with Jerusalem’s king Adoni-zedek to resist Israel — the name Adoni-zedek (“lord of righteousness/Zedek”) echoes Melchizedek (“king of righteousness/Zedek”), Abraham’s priestly benefactor. The contrast is deliberate in the narrative: where Melchizedek of Salem blessed Abraham (Genesis 14:18-20), Adoni-zedek of Jerusalem opposed Joshua. Christ is the true Melchizedek-king (Hebrews 7:1-3: “king of righteousness,” king of peace), whose defeat of every Adoni-zedek figure establishes the true kingly-priestly order.
13: A type: kings of Eglon, Gezer. Eglon (city) shares its name with the Moabite king in Judges 3:12-30, whose assassination by Ehud begins the first Judges cycle of deliverance. The king of Gezer is listed here but Gezer itself will not be fully taken — it passes to Israel only when Pharaoh gives it as a dowry with his daughter in Solomon’s time (1 Kings 9:16). The unfinished conquest of Gezer is one of the canonical examples of remaining Canaanite enclaves. Brief entries: each named king is a domain of opposition that Christ’s cosmic victory encompasses, including the delayed and the partially-resolved.
14: A type: kings of Debir and Geder. Debir (also called Kiriath-sepher, “city of the book/scribe”) will become the prize that Caleb’s nephew Othniel wins by conquering it, receiving Caleb’s daughter Achsah as his wife (Joshua 15:15-17; Judges 1:11-13). Othniel becomes the first judge of Israel. The king of Debir’s defeat here makes possible the subsequent victory that will generate Israel’s first deliverer — a layered typology where each conquest enables the next covenant act. The chain leads forward to Christ: each judge-deliverer in Israel’s history points toward the ultimate Judge and Deliverer.
15: A type: kings of Hormah and Arad. Hormah (“devoted to destruction”) was the site of Israel’s self-cursing vow in Numbers 21:2-3: “If you will give this people into my hand, I will devote their cities to destruction.” YHWH answered the vow and Israel devoted the Canaanites to destruction, naming the place Hormah. The listing of the king of Hormah here fulfills the covenant vow of Numbers 21, one of many narrative threads that Joshua’s victory list closes. Christ is the one in whom every covenant vow finds its Yes (2 Corinthians 1:20) — the pattern of promised destruction of the enemies of God’s people is fulfilled in him.
16: A type: kings of Libnah and Adullam. Adullam is the location of David’s cave-headquarters during his flight from Saul (1 Samuel 22:1) — the future site of the anointed king in exile becomes one of the 31 conquered domains. Every site that will figure in Israel’s covenant history — including the hiding places of the rejected-and-vindicated king — is already claimed in the conquest. The pattern of the anointed one who passes through a period of rejection and hiding before enthronement is the pattern of David and ultimately of Christ, whose death and descent precede his resurrection and exaltation.
17: A type: kings of Tappuah and Hepher. Brief entries in the victory catalog. These smaller city-kingdoms represent the comprehensive scope of the conquest — not merely the major cities but the minor administrative centers scattered through the hill country and lowlands. The thoroughness of the list reflects the thoroughness of the divine gift: the entire land in all its administrative complexity belongs to YHWH and is redistributed to his people. Revelation 11:15 — “The kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of his Christ” — is the eschatological fulfillment of the comprehensive conquest register.
18: A type: kings of Aphek and Lasharon. Aphek recurs in Israel’s history as a site of Philistine military operations — it is at Aphek that the Philistines encamp before the battle where the ark is captured (1 Samuel 4:1). The king of Aphek is listed among Joshua’s defeated, but Aphek will require further battles in subsequent generations. This is the pattern of the “already and not yet” in Israel’s conquest: the victory is established in principle and listed in the catalog, but experiential possession of individual sites requires ongoing faithfulness and engagement.
19: A type: kings of Madon and Hazor. Hazor is the great northern city-state — “formerly the head of all those kingdoms” (Joshua 11:10) — whose king Jabin led the northern coalition against Israel. Joshua burned Hazor completely. But a second king Jabin of Hazor will oppress Israel in Judges 4, requiring Deborah’s deliverance. The listing of Hazor’s defeated king here, while the city will require reconquest in the Judges period, illustrates the recurring-conquest pattern: the complete victory of the Christ-figure does not eliminate the need for subsequent spiritual warfare in each generation, though the ultimate outcome remains settled.
20: A type: kings of Shimron-meron and Achshaph. These northern cities were part of the Hazor coalition (Joshua 11:1). The northern coalition’s defeat made possible the settlement of Galilee — the region where Jesus conducted the majority of his ministry (Matthew 4:15-16: “Galilee of the Gentiles — the people dwelling in darkness have seen a great light”). The conquest of these cities created the space in which the Light of the world would eventually walk. The geography of Joshua’s victory is the geography of Christ’s ministry.
21: A type: kings of Taanach and Megiddo. Megiddo is the most prophetically significant site in this list. “Armageddon” in Revelation 16:16 is a Greek transliteration of Har Megiddo (Mountain/Hill of Megiddo) — the gathering place of the kings of the earth for the final battle against the returning Christ. The listing of the king of Megiddo among Joshua’s defeated anticipates the final “Megiddo” in Revelation 19:19-21, where the beast and the kings of the earth are gathered against the rider on the white horse and are defeated. Joshua’s conquest of Megiddo is the typological first entry in a long chain culminating in the eschatological victory.
22: A type: kings of Kedesh and Jokneam. Kedesh is one of the six cities of refuge (Joshua 20:7) — a city of asylum where one who killed unintentionally could flee from the avenger of blood. The city that provides refuge for the manslayer is among the conquered cities whose king is listed in the defeat catalog. This juxtaposition is theologically rich: the city of refuge (type of Christ as our refuge, our shelter from the consequences of sin) is established in a domain already conquered by the divine warrior. Christ as our city of refuge (Hebrews 6:18: “we who have fled for refuge might have strong encouragement to hold fast to the hope set before us”) inhabits a domain whose victory was established before we fled to it.
23: A type: king of Dor on the coast and king of Goyim in Gilgal. “Goyim” (nations/Gentiles) is a notable name — a king whose domain is named after the nations. The conquest of the king of Goyim in a land where Gentile peoples have sovereignty is the type of Christ’s universal kingship over all nations: “Ask of me, and I will make the nations (goyim) your heritage, and the ends of the earth your possession” (Psalms 2:8). The victory over the king of Goyim in Joshua’s day anticipates the Great Commission and Christ’s kingship over all goyim.
24: A type: thirty-one kings in all. The total — thirty-one kings, thirty-one domains of royal power brought under YHWH’s suzerainty through Joshua — is the victory register of the entire Canaanite campaign. The number thirty-one is not symbolic (unlike the seven-nation lists) but simply comprehensive: however many kings held sovereignty in Canaan, all are defeated and listed. The NT counterpart is Ephesians 1:21 — Christ is exalted “far above all rule and authority and power and dominion, and above every name that is named” — and Philippians 2:10-11: “at the name of Jesus every knee should bow, in heaven and on earth and under the earth, and every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is Lord.” The victory catalog of Joshua 12 is the shadow of the cosmic catalog of Christ’s universal lordship.
